title: Plan Board can now explicitly reconcile stale TFS planning dates from internal intent
impact: When the /planning/plan-board workspace detects stale, missing, or legacy-invalid TFS planning-date projection on an epic that already has internal planning intent, it now exposes one explicit per-epic "Reconcile TFS projection" action that rewrites both TFS planning-date fields together from the existing internal intent, refreshes the returned board diagnostics, and leaves the durable internal planning intent unchanged.
area: Planning / Plan Board / TFS Projection Reconciliation

title: Plan Board now surfaces planning recovery, drift, and calendar blocker diagnostics
impact: The /planning/plan-board workspace now shows when epic planning intent was recovered or normalized from TFS projected dates, highlights stale or missing TFS date projections against internal intent, surfaces legacy invalid projected dates and limited sprint-calendar coverage, and exposes clear blocker messaging when the canonical sprint calendar cannot be resolved safely instead of failing silently.
area: Planning / Plan Board / TFS Projection Diagnostics

title: Plan Board now uses the real product planning board with explicit epic-planning actions
impact: The /planning/plan-board workspace now loads the persisted product planning board for the selected product, renders the main lane plus derived parallel tracks, highlights changed and affected epics, surfaces planning validation issues directly on the board, and exposes explicit controls for move, spacing, parallelization, return-to-main, reorder, shift-plan, reload, and reset without using drag-and-drop or the old ReleasePlanning model.

title: API startup now fails fast if any managed endpoint skips data-source classification
impact: Managed API, health, and SignalR endpoints now declare their data-source mode classification directly in endpoint metadata, the middleware reads that metadata instead of relying primarily on string route matching, and startup validation now blocks the app immediately if a managed endpoint is missing or duplicates classification metadata, preventing new unclassified routes from reaching runtime.

title: Startup-state route now loads through the governed API data-source classifier
impact: The authoritative `/api/startup-state` startup gate is now explicitly classified as a live-allowed startup/configuration read, so first-load navigation no longer fails inside the data-source mode middleware before the startup-state contract can resolve Profiles, Sync Gate, blocked startup, or the requested route.

title: Startup routing now consumes one authoritative backend startup-state contract
impact: The root startup gate now calls a single /api/startup-state endpoint that validates the requested route, reconciles any browser profile hint on the server, evaluates sync validity server-side, and returns the final startup route directly, so the client no longer infers readiness from local profile or cache state and deep links remain preserved through one deterministic backend contract.

title: Startup now resolves one deterministic profile-and-sync gate before any route renders
impact: The client now withholds Router initialization behind one central startup gate, reconciles browser profile hints against the authoritative server selection before deciding readiness, and routes only after an atomic startup state resolution decides between Profiles, Sync Gate, the requested deep link, or a blocked startup page. Partially valid sync states such as warning-only or stale cache attempts no longer qualify as ready.

title: Startup routing now revalidates profile selection and sync readiness before opening workspace pages
impact: The app now resolves startup navigation through one central gate before any workspace route renders, so fresh sessions and deep links no longer fall through to /home when the browser has not validated a selected profile or when the selected profile still needs its first sync; invalid cached profile selections are cleared safely and users are routed into Profiles or Sync Gate with a preserved return path.

title: Core analytics read surfaces now enforce canonical data-state rendering systemically
impact: Pipeline Insights, PR Delivery Insights, Pull Request Insights, Portfolio Flow Trend, Sprint Execution, Product Roadmaps, and shared effort/capacity/flow/forecast panels now route read-state loading, empty, invalid-filter, and failure behavior through governed canonical data-state rendering instead of page-local fallback UI, and the read-surface governance audit now discovers covered pages and panels by pattern with explicit mutation-flow exclusions instead of relying on a fixed file allowlist.
area: Home / Planning / Delivery / Pull Requests / Pipelines / Metrics

title: More planning and health read surfaces now use one canonical data-state renderer
impact: Backlog Health, Multi-Product Planning, Sprint Activity, Plan Board, the dependency panel, and the timeline panel now route loading, empty, invalid-filter, and failure handling through DataStateViewModel<T> plus CanonicalDataStateView instead of local null checks, manual loading flags, or ad-hoc error UI, and new governance audits block those legacy patterns from returning on the governed read surfaces.

title: Canonical UI data-state normalization now removes page-level Not Ready handling
impact: Cache-backed pages and workspace tiles now normalize Not Ready responses into the governed loading state before UI rendering, plan-board and roadmap summaries reuse the same canonical fallback path as the rest of the app, and governance audits now block new page/component references to Not Ready state tokens or direct DataStatePanel usage.
area: Home / Planning / Delivery / Pull Requests / Pipelines / Validation

title: Shared data-state rendering now expands beyond trend pages
impact: Project Planning Overview, Health Overview, Bug Triage, Validation Queue, Validation Triage, Sprint Activity, and shared planning/health fallback panels now route loading, empty, invalid-filter, and failure messaging through one governed data-state view layer instead of mixing direct DataStatePanel usage with page-local fallback branches, and new governance tests now block pages from rendering DataStatePanel directly.

title: Trend pages now distinguish invalid filters, empty periods, and load failures
impact: Trend-facing pages and workspace signals now use one shared trend-state pattern so unresolved or rejected filters show an explicit invalid-filter state with highlighted global filter controls, valid zero-result periods show a no-data state, cache-not-ready responses normalize into loading instead of a separate UI state, partial pipeline sections no longer downgrade the whole page to empty, and backend/effective-filter divergences are logged in development instead of collapsing everything into generic unavailable messaging.

title: Trend pages now carry the shared sprint-range filter into backend trend reads consistently
impact: Delivery Trends, Portfolio Delivery, Portfolio Flow Trend, and shared PR trend reads now translate the resolved global From/To sprint selection through one shared client mapper before calling backend trend endpoints, so multi-sprint requests keep the intended sprint window instead of dropping the range and drifting to unrelated sprint data.

title: Shared collapsible filters now expand directly below their summary header
impact: Managed pages that use the shared global filter chrome now render the expanded filter body immediately under the filter summary instead of dropping it after the main page content, preserving the existing filter state, summary chips, and collapse/expand behavior across delivery, trends, planning, health, bug, and work-item pages.
area: Home / Delivery / Trends / Planning / Health / Bugs / Work Items

title: Client-side cache-backed reads now keep one explicit DataState model through shared services and UI fallbacks
impact: Work item, pull request, pipeline, release-planning, roadmap, and health/backlog client reads now preserve explicit Not Ready, Empty, Failed, and Invalid states instead of collapsing cache-backed responses into nulls or empty payloads, and shared DataState views now surface invalid filter scope as a warning state instead of treating corrected responses as ready success.
area: Home / Planning / Health / Work Items / Delivery

title: Cache-backed client services now preserve data-state and filter corrections
impact: Home and other cache-backed client flows now keep explicit Not Ready, Empty, Failed, and filter-correction states instead of collapsing them into null/default payloads, and Home workspace signals now reject invalid product scope before calling sprint-scoped endpoints while still using effective scoped products for all-products aggregation.

title: Home dashboard now reports cache-readiness cleanly and keeps delivery signals valid for all-products scope
impact: The Home product bar now shows the backend cache/data-state reason instead of a generic "Context metrics unavailable." failure when cached analytics are not ready, and the Delivery workspace tile now resolves sprint execution per product so the default all-products Home view no longer falls into an invalid request path and shows "Signal unavailable.".

title: PR delivery and sprint activity pages now use the shared global filter architecture end-to-end
impact: PR Delivery Insights and Sprint Activity now open and reload from the shared team and sprint filter state instead of maintaining page-local filters, and Pull Request Insights no longer uses its own fallback sprint recovery logic, so navigation between filtered pages keeps a single consistent effective filter state.

title: Shared team and sprint filters now stay valid during in-page filter changes
impact: Sprint Execution, Portfolio Delivery, Portfolio Flow Trend, Delivery Trends, and Pipeline Insights now re-resolve team and sprint changes through the shared filter layer immediately instead of relying on page-local resets or follow-up navigation timing, and shared team/sprint labels remain readable while Health Overview now surfaces canonical filter corrections when the backend normalizes filter scope.

title: Sprint-based pages now open with a valid shared filter state and readable filter labels
impact: Sprint Trends, Sprint Delivery, and other shared filter pages now auto-resolve a valid team and sprint window before their first query runs, keep the resolved defaults in the URL, and show readable team and sprint names in shared filter summaries and canonical filter notices instead of raw IDs.

title: TFS verification now validates analytics fields against recent work item samples
impact: Settings verification now checks analytics field metadata first and then inspects several recent non-Removed work items instead of relying on a single legacy sample, so fields that exist in TFS no longer fail verification just because they are absent or empty on one older item.

title: Hosted startup now serves the Blazor WebAssembly client assets correctly
impact: The hosted app at / now serves the client index and required _framework startup files from the API host again, so the browser no longer fails on missing blazor.webassembly.js, WebAssembly/PDB startup assets, or the invalid empty preload tag during startup.

title: Onboarding pipeline binding blockers now keep replacement intent across all fix-entry surfaces
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, invalid existing pipeline bindings now show replacement-focused next-step labels on blocker cards, root-cause cards, and top-blocker summaries, and every fix entry path opens the binding replacement flow instead of incorrectly falling back to create-binding mode.

title: Onboarding assignment fix links now stay in replacement mode for invalid team and pipeline bindings
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, invalid team and pipeline binding blockers now route into an explicit replacement intent that highlights the binding action zone, shows only replacement-selection UI, loads project-scoped replacement candidates without falling back to create-binding mode, and keeps users in a readable empty state when no replacement candidates are available.

title: Onboarding assignment blockers can now replace invalid team and pipeline bindings in place
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, team-assignment and pipeline-assignment blockers now load authoritative replacement candidates inside the binding action zone, route the selected replacement through the existing execution-intent mutation flow, update the binding through the onboarding execution service, and refresh graph plus problem counts so resolved blockers disappear without leaving the current onboarding workspace.

title: Onboarding fix links now open the correct binding editor and no longer present a contradictory read-only banner
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, binding-focused fix links now route team, pipeline, and product-root binding work into the binding action zone instead of the wrong mutation surface, and the workspace banner now clarifies that the graph remains read-only until a matching execution intent activates a write-capable action zone.

title: Onboarding fix flows now stay in the right workspace context and complete create actions reliably
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, onboarding no longer fails on unclassified onboarding API routes, create actions now treat valid created responses as success, and global project/root blockers now open the correct action zones with preserved parent context so connection and project fixes refresh the workspace as expected.

title: The onboarding workspace can now execute safe onboarding fixes through intent-first action zones
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, onboarding fix locations now open write-capable action zones for connection, project, team, pipeline, product-root, and binding mutations, validate input before calling the existing onboarding CRUD API, show explicit success or failure feedback, preserve the current execution context, and refresh the graph plus problem projections after each successful change.

title: The onboarding workspace now deep-links each suggested fix to the right read-only execution context
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, onboarding problems and root causes now expose deterministic execution intents, confidence labels, fix-location navigation with pre-selected connection/project/root context, highlighted graph targets, and non-interactive future action zones so users can move from guidance to the correct execution surface without any write behavior.

title: The onboarding workspace now explains the next step, impact, and shared root causes
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, the read-only onboarding workspace now maps visible onboarding problems to deterministic suggested actions, shows what each issue will unblock, groups repeated symptoms under shared root causes, and adds minimal action hints inside the graph without introducing any write or import behavior.

title: The onboarding workspace now highlights top blockers, reasons, and exact graph locations
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, the read-only onboarding workspace now adds a problem-first view, prioritized top blockers, scope-aware warning groups, clearer blocker/warning labels, and one-click navigation back into the graph so users can see what is broken, where it is broken, why it is broken, and what to inspect first without any write actions.

title: A new read-only onboarding workspace can surface onboarding graph status behind a feature flag
impact: When the OnboardingWorkspace feature flag is enabled, the client exposes a read-only onboarding workspace that shows onboarding status, blockers, warnings, connections, projects, teams, pipelines, product roots, bindings, and backend-filtered graph context without any write, import, or migration actions.

title: Global product selection now stays authoritative across planning, roadmap, backlog, bug, and work-item pages
impact: Project-scoped planning routes now constrain only which products are valid, the roadmap editor can bootstrap the shared product selection once from its route without continuing to override it, and invalid product/route combinations now show explicit invalid-context states instead of silently switching scope or falling back to empty results.

title: Portfolio Delivery and Pipeline Insights now honor the shared product filter
impact: Portfolio Delivery and Pipeline Insights now send the selected product scope through their canonical API filters instead of silently reading all products, so single-product analysis matches the shared filter state while all-products mode stays explicit when no product is selected.

title: Shared product, team, and sprint filters now allow only structurally valid combinations
impact: Planning, delivery, pipeline, pull-request, and build-quality reads now resolve product/team/sprint context through one canonical backend graph, incompatible combinations are rejected before handlers run, and the shared filter UI now limits team choices to the selected product so invalid team-sprint combinations cannot be created from the client.

title: Planning and sprint pages now reject inconsistent Battleship context earlier
impact: Sprint metrics and Sprint Execution now fail fast when sprint scope, team scope, and product scope do not align, shared defaults no longer jump to another product's team when an explicitly selected product has no sprint history, and Battleship mock validation now checks sprint definitions and known team assignments to catch drift before pages fall back to misleading empty states.

title: Battleship planning and execution pages now render current sprint and project data consistently
impact: Sprint Execution now shows Battleship Sprint 11 scope instead of an empty state, project planning overview now deserializes and renders the Battleship project summary correctly, Multi-Product Planning now uses real team sprint cadence with visible product lanes, and Plan Board restores sprint columns for the selected product teams.

title: Product selection now stays global across planning, health, bug, and work-item pages
impact: Home, Backlog Health, Plan Board, Product Roadmaps, Product Roadmap Editor, Bugs Triage, and Work Item Explorer now read product scope from the shared global filter only. Product-scoped pages stop silently picking the first or profile-owned product, direct roadmap-editor routes no longer override the shared selection, and pages that require a single product now wait for an explicit global product choice instead of falling back implicitly.

title: Planning and delivery pages now keep route context and open with meaningful defaults
impact: Project- and product-scoped planning routes now keep their path authority, delivery and planning pages now initialize team/sprint/product defaults from the battleship mock portfolio on first load, and shared filter controls moved below the primary page signal so analytical content stays above the fold.

title: Battleship mock mode now fills roadmap, build quality, and bug-triage pages with realistic data
impact: Mock startup now begins in a ready-to-sync state, battleship work items now use TFS-style tags with roadmap and triage coverage, and bug triage plus roadmap pages load richer seeded content instead of empty catalogs.

title: Cache-backed planning and analytics services now use one generated-client data-state contract
impact: Build quality, planning summary, release planning, pipeline, pull request, metric, and validation reads now flow through generated API clients with consistent ready, empty, not-ready, and failed state handling, reducing stale direct-route behavior and preserving cache state transitions across the client.

title: Configuration export now completes reliably on SQLite-backed development environments
impact: Settings export no longer fails when the API orders DateTimeOffset-backed records on SQLite, so configuration export returns successfully and keeps its chronological ordering behavior intact.

title: Cold-cache pages now use one consistent not-ready and failure UX
impact: Health, planning, trend, delivery, and workspace pages now treat cold-cache states as first-class UI states with consistent 'Data not ready' and 'Data unavailable' handling, retry actions, and tile badges, so pages stop falling through to misleading no-data or cleared-state behavior while cache-backed data is still warming.

title: Cache-backed build quality and planning pages now show readiness states instead of crashing
impact: Health Overview, Sprint Delivery build quality, Pipeline Insights overlays, Trends pipeline signals, and project planning summary pages now understand the backend cache envelope contract, so cache-not-ready responses show explicit recovery states instead of JSON errors, empty fallback metrics, or missing summary cards.

title: Startup now blocks invalid readiness states before loading workspaces
impact: The app no longer treats unknown startup state as usable: root startup now routes setup, profile selection, sync-required, and backend-unavailable cases explicitly, guarded workspace routes stop rendering when readiness is not confirmed, and startup failures now show one blocking recovery screen instead of falling through into broken pages.

title: Filtering now uses one shared entry point across home and planning pages
impact: Home and planning pages now remove duplicate team, sprint, range, project, and product filter panels so users change shared filter context only through the global summary bar and global filter controls, while page-specific controls remain only for local analytical options or explicit range projections.

title: Global filters now enforce required context and expose shared controls
impact: Home and planning pages now use one interactive global filter surface with explicit unresolved and invalid messaging, per-filter reset actions, centralized route correction, and query gating so pages stop loading data until the active filter state is valid for that page.

title: Global filters now keep stable canonical route and time semantics
impact: Home pages now derive team, sprint, product, project, and range context from one canonical filter state with explicit resolved, unresolved, or invalid classification, normalized inclusive sprint ranges, and guarded route synchronization to prevent redundant rewrites during refresh and navigation.

title: Global filters now normalize one shared canonical state
impact: Home and planning pages now route product, project, team, and time context through one shared filter store, so the read-only filter summary reflects normalized canonical state while existing links, query parameters, and page behavior continue to work.

title: Pages now show a read-only filter summary bar
impact: Home and planning pages now expose a passive summary of detected product, project, team, and time context for observability only. The bar does not change navigation, queries, or page behavior.

title: Legacy workspace navigation and fallback sprint cadence were removed
impact: The old /legacy and /workspace navigation model is no longer available in the shipped client, and planning timeline views now show cadence as unavailable instead of assuming a default sprint duration when no completed sprint history exists.

title: Delivery and trends pages now require explicit sprint context
impact: Legacy `/legacy` and `/workspace/*` routes are no longer directly routeable, Sprint Delivery / Sprint Execution / Pipeline Insights / Portfolio Delivery stop auto-selecting team or sprint context, PR and trend pages no longer anchor themselves to an inferred current sprint, and What's New Since Last Sync is now explicitly treated as an operational page outside the global filter contract.

title: Configuration import now restores products under a valid project
impact: Importing saved configuration now recreates products with the required project relationship, so settings import no longer fails after the repository-wide persistence integrity checks were tightened.

title: Analytical client surfaces now render only from explicit data states
impact: Forecast, Backlog Health, Effort Distribution, Capacity Calibration, Plan Board, Trends, Bug Insights, and Add Lane now consume cache-backed data through explicit data-state responses instead of raw cache DTO calls and exception-driven UI fallbacks.

title: Cache-backed analytics now use one explicit data-state contract
impact: Cache-backed analytical reads now use the canonical route plus an explicit DataState contract instead of mixing raw routes, /state routes, and middleware cache-not-ready transport errors. Several analytics pages now render syncing and failure states from that single contract.

title: Cache-backed direct-entry pages now render explicit sync states
impact: Bug Detail, Bugs Triage, Validation Triage, Validation Queue, Validation Fix, Sprint Activity, and Portfolio Flow now render explicit syncing, empty, available, or failed states instead of discovering cache readiness through HTTP errors or recovery-first UX.

title: Direct-entry bug and validation routes now guide missing context safely
impact: Opening Bug Detail, Validation Queue, or Validation Fix directly without the right URL context no longer shows raw parameter or transport errors; each route now explains what is missing or invalid and points back to the correct recovery page.

title: Targeted direct routes now fail safely instead of crashing
impact: Directly opening Bugs Triage, Sprint Activity, and Portfolio Progress no longer triggers the Blazor crash screen or raw server exception text; the routes now either load normally or show clear recovery states when cache-backed data is unavailable.

title: Planning now highlights likely cross-product team collisions
impact: Multi-Product Planning can now show optional capacity collision hints when overlapping epic windows across selected products involve the same mapped teams, adding a non-blocking shared-axis signal without calculating overload or changing forecasts.

title: Planning now shows multi-product timelines on one shared axis
impact: Multi-Product Planning now lets product owners compare selected products on one shared time axis, toggle cross-product end-date clusters, and see delayed or missing-forecast signals per product without adding new backend planning logic.

title: Planning timelines now use real sprint cadence per product
impact: Product Roadmaps and Multi-Product Planning now derive epic start ranges from each product team's recent sprint history instead of assuming a fixed 14-day sprint, while still falling back to the current sprint or a default duration when no usable sprint history exists.

title: Product roadmaps now show projected completion dates and simple sequence bars
impact: Planning roadmaps now load persisted forecast projections per product, show each roadmap epic's projected completion date as the primary signal with paired sprint count as secondary context, highlight overdue epics with delay duration, and render each roadmap epic independently on a shared per-product timeline axis so overlaps and gaps stay visible without recalculating forecast logic in the UI.

title: Planning now shows project-level overview signals
impact: Project-scoped planning routes now include a read-only overview page plus project summary bars on Plan Board and Product Roadmaps, showing planned versus unplanned PBIs, capacity-per-sprint, and imbalance or overcommit signals without changing write behavior.

title: Planning now supports project aliases and project-first selection
impact: Plan Board and Product Roadmaps now accept project-alias URLs, show a project selector above the product context, and keep existing productId planning routes working for backward compatibility.

title: Backlog health iteration cards now show counted issues
impact: Backlog Health iteration cards now show the total number of issues counted in each health-score calculation, so product owners can see how many blocked, parent-progress, and missing-effort issues drove the score without leaving the current view.

title: Workspace pages now keep Build Quality, metrics, and active context stable
impact: Health Overview and Sprint Delivery now load Build Quality data without client deserialization failures, no-data sprint and backlog analytics now return successful empty responses instead of 404s, and product/team/sprint context now stays attached while moving between Home, Health, Delivery, Trends, and Planning pages.

title: App shell and guarded APIs now share explicit route handling
impact: Opening /, client-side workspace routes, SignalR setup paths, TFS validation endpoints, and build-quality APIs no longer fail route classification during startup, while unknown managed API routes still stay blocked until they are explicitly categorized.

title: PR analytics now wait for cached sync state
impact: Pull request analytics and related cache-backed workspace reads now return an explicit cache-not-ready response until the active profile has completed a successful sync, while onboarding and discovery endpoints stay live-capable.

title: Pipeline filters now keep the correct latest runs
impact: Pipeline metrics, pipeline run views, and build-quality calculations now apply branch and time filters before truncating cached results so scoped runs are no longer displaced by unrelated activity.

title: Canonical filter warnings now reach the UI
impact: PR, pipeline, delivery, and sprint views now show when requested filters were invalid or normalized instead of silently applying backend fallback scope.

title: Pipeline run filters no longer hide quiet pipelines
impact: Pipeline metrics and filtered run views now keep valid runs from less-active pipelines instead of dropping them when busier pipelines have more recent cached activity.

title: Sprint analytics now use canonical filter scope
impact: Sprint metrics, backlog health, multi-iteration health, capacity plan, sprint trend, sprint execution, and sprint activity endpoints now resolve requested versus effective sprint scope once at the API boundary and return that metadata in migrated responses.

title: Delivery analytics now use canonical filter scope
impact: Delivery progress, capacity calibration, portfolio delivery, home product bar, and delivery build-quality endpoints now resolve requested versus effective product and time scope once at the API boundary and return that metadata in migrated responses.

title: Pipeline analytics now use canonical filter scope
impact: Pipeline metrics, pipeline runs, and pipeline insights now resolve requested versus effective product, repository, and time scope once at the API boundary and return that metadata in each migrated response envelope.

title: PR analytics now use canonical filter resolution
impact: Pull Request metrics, filtered lists, sprint trends, insights, and delivery insights now resolve requested versus effective product, team, repository, and time scope once at the API boundary and return that metadata in every response envelope.

title: Portfolio reads now echo canonical filter resolution
impact: Portfolio read responses now return requested versus effective filter scope and invalid filter fields so later UI work can surface deterministic backend filtering.

title: Portfolio CDC history labels are now easier to scan
impact: Portfolio Progress now shortens comparison context, disambiguates repeated sprint labels in history, removes leftover helper copy, and replaces cryptic changed-row shorthand with explicit reasons.

title: Portfolio CDC history now shows corrected delta cues
impact: Portfolio Progress now rounds CDC deltas consistently, merges arrows into the delta display, clarifies comparison context, and makes changed work package reasons easier to scan.

title: Portfolio CDC history now leads with decisions
impact: Portfolio Progress now opens with one compact summary, one primary decision signal, a scan-first trend overview, and collapsible secondary CDC sections.

title: Portfolio CDC history view is easier to scan
impact: Portfolio Progress now groups CDC signals, shows snapshot deltas more clearly, and separates active versus retired work package changes for faster review.

title: Mock portfolio history now uses battleship snapshots
impact: Mock mode now seeds a realistic battleship CDC snapshot timeline so Portfolio Progress can show empty-to-complete portfolio history without a separate test dataset.

title: Portfolio CDC now honors empty and single-snapshot history
impact: Portfolio trends and signals now preserve empty-owner snapshots and honor an explicit one-snapshot history request without inventing comparison deltas.

title: Portfolio CDC now shows persisted trends and signals
impact: Portfolio Progress now shows persisted snapshot history, explicit earlier-snapshot comparison, archived-history visibility, and deterministic decision signals without any UI-side CDC recalculation.

title: Portfolio Progress now includes CDC snapshot views
impact: Portfolio Progress now shows read-only CDC snapshot rows, snapshot comparison deltas, lifecycle state, and output filtering without adding any UI-side portfolio calculations.

title: Error recovery button now uses sentence case
impact: The global error screen now labels its recovery action as "Try again" for consistent UI wording.

title: Epic progress now uses deterministic feature rollups
impact: Sprint Delivery epic progress now comes only from weighted Feature progress, ignores non-Feature children and epic overrides, and returns 0% when no Feature weight is available.

title: Feature progress inputs are now canonicalized
impact: Sprint Delivery now ignores invalid TimeCriticality overrides, uses canonical work item typing on the feature-progress path, and keeps feature progress calculation details internal to the backend progress engine.

title: Feature progress now follows Phase B CDC rules
impact: Sprint Delivery feature progress now uses deterministic child effort for PBIs and bugs, ignores tasks, excludes removed scope, and applies TimeCriticality as a strict override.

title: Sprint Delivery now shows canonical analytics
impact: Sprint Delivery now shows canonical product, epic, and feature progress, forecast, delta, planning quality, and insight values directly from backend analytics without UI recalculation.

title: Feature progress now uses one canonical engine
impact: Feature progress now comes from one backend calculation that applies TimeCriticality overrides, clamps invalid override values, and preserves empty-scope features for downstream delivery views.

title: Trends workspace tiles now show live signal badges
impact: The Trends workspace now shows lightweight PR, bug, and pipeline signal badges with graceful fallback states so you can see where attention is needed before opening a detail page.

title: Health routes and labels are now consistent
impact: Health now uses consistent Overview and Backlog Health labels, with Backlog Health living at /home/health/backlog-health while the old backlog URL still works for bookmarks.

title: Health workspace now opens as a hub
impact: Health now opens as a navigation hub, while the existing Build Quality page moved to Health Overview at /home/health/overview.

title: Mock Health Build Quality now shows seeded metrics
impact: Mock-mode Health now shows realistic builds, tests, coverage, and confidence values for Incident Response Control after a normal sync.

title: Health now shows overall Build Quality summary
impact: Health now shows the combined rolling Build Quality summary with confidence, alongside the existing per-product quality cards.

title: Build Quality sync now loads test runs and coverage
impact: Build Quality imports now use TFS-compatible test-run and coverage requests so synced builds populate test runs and coverage metrics again.

title: Build Quality now appears across Health and Delivery
impact: Health now opens with a Build Quality overview, Sprint Delivery shows sprint-scoped Build Quality, and Pipeline Insights adds Build Quality indicators and drawer details for selected builds.

title: Onboarding import now completes trusted setup
impact: Importing a saved configuration during onboarding now finishes the wizard automatically and preserves trusted TFS readiness so restart no longer sends verified imports back to TFS settings.

title: Sprint Delivery breadcrumbs now restore drill-down state
impact: Sprint Delivery drill-down breadcrumbs now return to the portfolio, product, or epic view on the same page instead of navigating away from the current sprint flow.

title: Planner sync baseline now works with SQLite
impact: Work item sync no longer fails against SQLite-backed local data, and planner diagnostics now log sampled IDs so sync validation is easier to inspect.

title: PR delivery scatter legend now matches chart
impact: The PR Delivery Insights scatter chart now uses readable active-PR colors and a right-side legend whose status colors and epic shapes match the points shown in the chart.

title: TFS mode now blocks mixed mock and real runtime
impact: Settings and sync startup now fail fast when real-data mode would resolve mock TFS services, preventing blank settings flows and silent mixed-provider behavior.

title: Mock mode now seeds PR and pipeline sources
impact: Mock-backed startup now seeds repository-backed pull request and pipeline source data so demo syncs and analytics no longer start with empty PR and pipeline counts.

title: Mock startup now seeds cache-sync configuration
impact: Fresh mock-backed startup now seeds the Azure DevOps connection used by sprint projections so onboarding data, cache sync, and analytics pages work without manual configuration first.

title: Mock mode now seeds realistic onboarding data
impact: Mock mode now starts with seeded product owners, products, teams, and more varied backlog and sprint history so onboarding and analytics views show usable non-flat demo data immediately.

title: Portfolio flow now uses canonical story points
impact: Portfolio flow trend now reads canonical stock, remaining scope, inflow, throughput, and completion from PortfolioFlow projections so the page no longer shows effort-based proxy semantics as points.

title: Delivery labels now separate story points and hours
impact: Forecast, sprint delivery, roadmap, and portfolio views now label story-point metrics separately from effort hours so delivery summaries no longer present hours as points.

title: Percentile metrics now use one interpolation rule
impact: Planning, pipeline, pull request, and bug percentile metrics now use the same linear-interpolation percentile semantics so percentile-based summaries stay consistent across views.

title: Effort diagnostics now use stable effort-hour rules
impact: Effort imbalance and concentration analytics now describe fixed stable semantics, keep concentration bands deterministic, and label effort values consistently as effort hours instead of points.

title: Sprint execution now reports canonical SP rates
impact: Sprint execution summaries now expose committed, added, removed, delivered, and spillover story-point totals plus churn and completion rates based on canonical PBI sprint rules.

title: Capacity calibration now uses story points
impact: Capacity calibration and planning guidance now measure sprint velocity with delivered PBI story points while exposing effort only as a diagnostic hours-per-story-point metric.

title: Sprint trend projections now store story points
impact: Sprint trend metrics now keep planned, delivered, and spillover story-point totals plus derived and unestimated-delivery diagnostics separately from effort hours.

title: Forecast rollups now use canonical story points
impact: Epic forecasts and Sprint Trend progress now roll up canonical PBI story points with parent fallback, derived fractional estimates, and bug/task exclusion instead of mixing effort hours into scope.

title: Sprint story point totals now use canonical PBIs
impact: Sprint planned and completed story-point totals now count only canonical PBI estimates with BusinessValue fallback, excluding bugs, tasks, and unestimated deliveries from velocity.

title: Work item sync now keeps story points separate
impact: Cached work items now preserve source story points independently from effort hours and business value so future planning metrics can use the original estimation fields correctly.

title: Sprint velocity now uses historical sprint rules
impact: Sprint metrics and forecast velocity now reconstruct committed sprint scope from day-two history and count delivery from the first canonical Done transition in the sprint window instead of today's sprint assignment.

title: Sprint spillover now follows canonical rules
impact: Sprint execution and sprint trend projections now count spillover only for committed PBIs that were not done at sprint end and moved directly into the next sprint.

title: Sprint commitment now uses day-two history
impact: Sprint planned scope, added scope, and removed scope now reconstruct commitment from the item's sprint membership one day after sprint start instead of using its current iteration assignment.

title: Sprint delivery now uses first Done timing
impact: Sprint delivery and sprint execution completion now count a work item only when its first canonical Done transition happens in the sprint window, preventing reopened items from being counted twice or reassigned by current state.

title: Configuration import now tracks entity results
impact: Configuration import now records structured per-entity results for profiles, products, teams, repository links, and settings so follow-up diagnostics can be more precise.

title: Configuration import now blocks duplicate actions
impact: Configuration import now validates JSON immediately, disables file selection plus repeat clicks while the import runs, shows staged progress text, and lets you reveal hidden clean-success details on demand.

title: Configuration import now runs as one guided flow
impact: Import from Settings and the first onboarding step now validates automatically when you click Import configuration, asks before wiping existing local configuration, and only keeps the large results panel for non-clean imports that need inspection.

title: Settings can import and export configuration
impact: You can now export the current Azure DevOps/TFS configuration to JSON and later validate plus import it again from Settings or the first onboarding step.

title: Sprint Delivery now fills progressively
impact: Sprint Delivery now shows its shell immediately, loads portfolio metrics first, and only loads deeper drilldown tables when you open them.

title: Health workspace now fills cards progressively
impact: The Health workspace now shows its page shell immediately and lets validation signals plus each product card load independently with local placeholders instead of waiting for the whole dashboard.

title: Home now fills widgets progressively
impact: The Home page now renders its shell immediately and lets workspace tiles, product context, and sync status load independently with local placeholders instead of waiting behind one page-level loading state.

title: Top workspace menu now reads as navigation
impact: The top workspace menu now uses a lightweight tab-like navigation bar with colored active workspace highlighting instead of outlined action buttons.

title: Workspace buttons now follow shared roles
impact: Home, workspace hubs, and top-bar actions now use the shared CompactButton roles so buttons stay compact and visually secondary to navigation tiles and cards.

title: Delivery and Planning hubs now match Home
impact: The Delivery and Planning hub pages now use the same standard navigation tiles and cleaner section hierarchy as Home, so it is easier to scan the next workspace to open.

title: Navigation tiles show clear interaction states
impact: Home and Trends navigation tiles now show visible hover, keyboard focus, and pressed feedback because the shared card shell styling applies directly to the rendered card root.

title: Home tiles now focus on investigation cues
impact: Home workspace tiles now show one short investigation-focused reason to navigate and no longer embed mini trend sparklines, so the workspace hub reads as navigation instead of a status dashboard.

title: Home shows sync status above workspaces
impact: The Home dashboard now places a compact last-sync row with Sync and What's new since sync actions above the workspace tiles so Product Owners can refresh data and review recent changes without pushing navigation below the fold.

title: Home workspace tiles use the standard layout
impact: Home workspace tiles now use the same centered NavigationTileCard layout as Trends, so the four workspace entry points read consistently while keeping their signal text and optional sparklines.

title: Home and Trends cards now share one shell
impact: Home workspace cards and Trends navigation cards now use the same clickable card shell, so hover, focus, pressed, and keyboard behavior match across both pages.

title: Home workspace tiles — interaction polish and micro-trend sparklines
impact: Workspace tiles now have a smooth hover lift (translateY -2px), a pressed feedback effect (translateY +1px), and a visible focus outline for keyboard navigation. Each tile may also display a small SVG sparkline indicator below the signal text when sufficient historical sprint data is available: the Delivery tile shows velocity trend (Done effort per sprint, up to 7 sprints), and the Trends tile shows bug-rate trend. Sparklines are amber-tinted when a deteriorating trend is detected. No axes, labels, or chart libraries are used.

title: Home product bar now shows context metrics
impact: The Home product bar now shows team sprint progress plus bug and changes-today counts, with bug and change totals filtered by the selected product chip.

title: Home tiles now show workspace signals
impact: Each Home workspace tile now shows one highest-priority signal in the active product context so Product Owners can open the right workspace faster.

title: Home dashboard redesigned for faster navigation
impact: The Home page now centers on four dominant workspace tiles, a horizontal product bar, lighter quick actions, and a compact sync footer so Product Owners can navigate by workspace first.

title: Plan Board shows active epic hierarchy
impact: The Plan Board backlog tree now keeps active Epics and Features visible in TFS backlog order even when they do not yet have plannable PBIs or bugs.

title: Plan Board can refresh from TFS
impact: The Plan Board now includes a Refresh from TFS action that reloads the selected product hierarchy into cache and redraws the board with the latest sprint assignments.

title: Plan Board now plans PBIs and bugs
impact: Plan Board drag-and-drop now assigns PBIs and bugs to upcoming sprints and the documentation reflects that TFS iteration changes are applied to those backlog items.

title: Release notes button added to profile selection
impact: The profile selection landing page now includes a What's New button so you can read release notes before entering a workspace.

title: Plan Board for sprint planning
impact: New Plan Board at /planning/plan-board enables drag-and-drop planning of PBIs and bugs into upcoming sprints per product.

title: Legacy /workspace/planning redirect removed
impact: The legacy Planning workspace route has been removed entirely; Planning is now accessed only via /home/planning.

title: Legacy Plan Board removed from Planning workspace
impact: The legacy Plan Board page and its backend have been removed. The Planning workspace now shows a placeholder for a future replacement board.

title: Planning workspace redesigned as navigation hub
impact: The Planning landing page now loads instantly as a lightweight navigation hub with cards for Product Roadmaps and Plan Board, replacing the previous heavy analytics page with velocity charts, forecast tables and capacity calculations.

title: Roadmap overview now links to product editor
impact: Each product lane on Product Roadmaps now shows an Edit roadmap action that opens the correct per-product roadmap editor without making the overview itself editable.

title: Roadmap epics now preserve TFS order on refresh
impact: Roadmap overview and editor now keep using TFS backlog priorities for epic discovery and reordering, so roadmap membership and ordering stay aligned after refresh unless priorities must be recovered.

title: Product lanes now swap real TFS priorities
impact: Moving a product earlier or later now swaps the neighbouring Objective backlog priorities exactly as stored in TFS, preserving the surrounding roadmap order after refresh.

title: Roadmap Analytics and Health Signals
impact: Roadmap epic cards now display effort totals, PBI counts, sprint estimates, velocity warnings, refinement/validation health signals, epic age, and confidence indicators.

title: Roadmap Snapshots — Persistent Database Storage
impact: Roadmap snapshots are now stored in the application database instead of browser localStorage, making them durable across sessions and devices.

title: Roadmap Snapshots and Drift Detection
impact: Product Roadmaps overview now supports creating snapshots to capture roadmap state and comparing them with the current roadmap to detect drift (reordered, added, or removed epics).

title: Roadmap Reporting and Export
impact: Product Roadmaps overview now offers reporting actions: visual Markdown roadmap, structured JSON export, and AI prompt templates for roadmap visualization — all copied to clipboard.

title: Product Roadmap Editor — UX Enhancements
impact: The Product Roadmap Editor now shows save feedback (Saving/Saved/Failed status chip), loading indicators during reload, improved empty roadmap guidance, roadmap size indicator, highlight animation for newly added epics, reserved metadata area on cards, and sticky column headers.

title: Product Roadmap Editor — Drag-and-Drop Support
impact: The Product Roadmap Editor now supports drag-and-drop for adding, removing, and reordering roadmap epics using MudBlazor DropContainer. Each epic card has a dedicated drag handle to prevent accidental drags. Drop zones provide visual feedback during drag operations. All existing button-based controls remain fully functional. All drag-and-drop changes are persisted to TFS and refreshed through the cache.

title: Pull Request Insights — Deferred items: Azure DevOps deep-link + Sprint quick-select
impact: Two deferred items from the original PR Insights implementation now resolved. (1) Azure DevOps deep-link: every PR scatter point, Top-3 friction card, and Longest-PR table row now includes a direct URL to the corresponding pull request in Azure DevOps/TFS. The URL is constructed server-side from TfsConfig.Url + TfsConfig.Project + RepositoryName + PrId (format: {url}/{project}/_git/{repo}/pullrequest/{id}). The PR Detail Drawer gains an “Open in Azure DevOps” button at the bottom. Top-3 cards show an “Open in Azure DevOps” inline link. Longest-PR table titles become clickable links. All links open in a new tab. The URL is null (and the button/link is hidden) when TFS config is unavailable — no breaking change for offline environments. New PrScatterPointDto.Url and PrProblematicEntryDto.Url properties added. 4 new unit tests covering: URL present with config, null without config, correct format, and trailing slash stripping. (2) Sprint quick-select: when a team is selected, a Sprint (optional) dropdown appears in the filter panel. Choosing a sprint sets the From/To date range to that sprint's boundaries and triggers a reload — equivalent to selecting the sprint as a custom date range. Selecting “Custom range” or manually editing the date pickers clears the sprint selection. The sprint list is loaded from the existing SprintService and sorted newest-first. No backend changes — pure UI enhancement that maps sprint boundaries to the existing date-range filter.

title: Pull Request Insights — Phase 3: Author Drill-Down Filter + Date Quick-Presets
impact: Two UX improvements to the Pull Request Insights page (/home/pull-requests). (1) Author drill-down filter: clicking any row in the Author Breakdown table (Section 6) now filters the PR scatter chart (Section 3) to show only that author's PRs. Non-matching points are heavily dimmed (opacity 0.12), providing immediate visual focus on the author's workflow pattern. An author-filter chip appears in the scatter chart header with a × close button. Clicking the same row again, the chip, or changing any server-side filter all clear the author filter. The active row in the Author Breakdown table is highlighted for clarity. PullRequestScatterSvg gains a new FilteredAuthor parameter that works independently of the existing HighlightedPrId parameter (both can be active at once: a point must match both to be fully opaque). (2) Date quick-presets: five chip buttons (1M / 3M / 6M / 1Y / 2Y) appear beneath the date pickers. Clicking a chip sets the From/To dates accordingly and triggers a reload. The active preset chip is highlighted in primary color. All changes are pure frontend — no backend or DTO changes.

title: Pull Request Insights — Phase 2: PR Detail Drawer + Per-Author Breakdown
impact: Two additions to the Pull Request Insights page (/home/pull-requests). (1) PR Detail Drawer: clicking a scatter point now opens a right-anchored MudDrawer showing full PR details — status chip (Merged clean / Merged rework / Abandoned / Active), PR title, author, repository, creation date, lifetime, review cycles (with 'rework' badge when > 1), files changed, and comment count. The drawer closes via the × button or automatically when filters change. This replaces the previous highlight-only click behaviour (highlight still works in parallel). (2) Author Breakdown: a new collapsible Section 6 shows per-author PR workflow statistics — PR count, merge %, abandon %, rework % (highlighted in orange > 30%), median lifetime, and average review cycles. Sorted by PR count descending. Answers the question: 'Which author's PRs show the most friction patterns?' Backend: new PrAuthorBreakdownDto added to PoTool.Shared.PullRequests; AuthorBreakdown list added to PullRequestInsightsDto; handler updated with author grouping (step 10). 3 new unit tests covering: breakdown sort order, rate computation, and empty state.

title: Pull Request Insights — Full overhaul: PO-first friction analysis page
impact: The Pull Request Insights page (/home/pull-requests) has been completely overhauled into a team-centric, PO-focused PR workflow friction page. All data comes from the local cache only — no live Azure DevOps calls. The page answers the question: 'Which pull requests indicate delivery friction in the team?' New layout has five sections: (1) Global summary chips — Total PRs, Merge %, Abandon %, Rework %, Median lifetime, P90 lifetime. (2) Top 3 friction PRs — cards ranked by composite score (lifetime 40%, review cycles 30%, files changed 20%, comments 10%); clicking highlights the PR in the scatter chart. (3) PR scatter chart — new PullRequestScatterSvg component (pure SVG, no JavaScript); X = creation date, Y = lifetime in hours; color by outcome (green = merged clean, yellow = merged after rework, red = abandoned); shape by repository; hover tooltip; click to highlight/dim; median and P90 overlay lines. (4) Longest PR table — top 20 by lifetime descending with columns: title, repository, author, lifetime, review cycles, files changed, comments, status. (5) Repository breakdown — collapsible per-repository table with merge %, abandon %, median/P90 lifetime, and average review cycles. Filters: team selector (scopes to team-linked products), date range (default last 6 months), optional repository filter. Backend: new GET api/PullRequests/insights endpoint; new GetPullRequestInsightsQueryHandler with 13 unit tests. 'Changes requested' is a proxy: since reviewer vote history is not stored in the cache, IterationCount > 1 is used as the rework signal.

title: Pipeline Insights — Phase 4: Auto-Sprint Selection + Scroll-to-Product Navigation
impact: Two UX improvements to the Pipeline Insights page. (1) Auto-sprint selection: when a team is selected, the current sprint (the sprint window covering today, or the most recently ended sprint as fallback) is automatically selected and insights are loaded immediately — no extra click required. (2) Scroll-to-product: clicking a card in the Global Top Pipelines In Trouble section now smoothly scrolls the page to the corresponding per-product section. A tooltip on each card hints at this interaction. Both changes are pure frontend (no backend or DTO changes).

title: Pipeline Insights — Phase 3: Per-Pipeline Breakdown Table + Half-Sprint Trend
impact: Per-product pipeline breakdown table added to the Pipeline Insights page. Each product section now renders a collapsible PipelineBreakdownTable component showing all pipelines (not just the top 3) with columns: Pipeline, Runs, Success%, Failure%, Median duration, P90, Δ Failure vs previous sprint, and a Half-Sprint Trend chip. Trend is computed by splitting the sprint at its midpoint and comparing failure rates: Improving (second half ≥ 10 pp better), Degrading (≥ 10 pp worse), Stable (< 10 pp difference), or Insufficient (< 2 completed runs in a half). Tooltips show actual first-half and second-half failure rates. Breakdown is ordered failure rate descending. Backend updated: PipelineBreakdownEntryDto and PipelineHalfSprintTrend enum added to PoTool.Shared.Pipelines; PipelineBreakdown list added to ProductPipelineInsightsDto; handler updated with BuildPipelineBreakdown helper. swagger.json + ApiClient.g.cs updated. 8 new unit tests covering: populated breakdown, correct metrics, failure-rate ordering, and all 4 half-sprint trend states.

title: Pipeline Insights — Phase 2: Pipeline Stability Scatter (TimeScatterSvg) + Build Summary Drawer
impact: Per-product pipeline build stability scatter chart added to the Pipeline Insights page. Each product section now renders a TimeScatterSvg component (Components/Charts/): X axis = build start time within the sprint, Y axis = build duration in minutes, dots colored by result (green=Succeeded, yellow=PartiallySucceeded, red=Failed, gray=Canceled). Overlay lines: Median duration (dashed blue), P90 duration (dashed amber), optional SLO line (solid red). Clicking a scatter dot opens a right-side Build Summary Drawer showing build number, pipeline name, result, start/finish time, duration, branch, and Azure DevOps link. Clicking a pipeline in the top-3 trouble list highlights its dots (dims others). A new optional 'SLO duration (min)' input in the context header applies a SLO line to all scatter charts. Backend updated: ProductPipelineInsightsDto now includes ScatterPoints (PipelineScatterPointDto list) and PipelineInsightsDto now includes SprintStart/SprintEnd boundaries. New DTOs, handler updates, swagger.json and ApiClient.g.cs updated. 5 new unit tests for scatter point generation.

title: Pipeline Insights — Phase 1: PO-first sprint stability overview per product
impact: New page at /home/pipeline-insights provides a PO-facing pipeline health view for a single sprint, using only cached data (no TFS calls). Delivers: global summary chips (total builds, failure rate, warning rate, P90 duration), global top-3 pipelines in trouble ranked by failure rate, per-product sections each showing a product-level top-3 in trouble and summary chips (failure rate, warning rate, success rate, median/P90 duration). Delta versus the immediately preceding sprint shown per pipeline (n/a when no previous sprint data). Two toggles: Include partial success (default ON) and Include canceled (default OFF). Accessible from the Trends (Past) workspace as a new 'Pipeline Insights' card. Backend: new GET api/Pipelines/insights endpoint, new GetPipelineInsightsQuery/Handler, new DTOs (PipelineInsightsDto, ProductPipelineInsightsDto, PipelineTroubleEntryDto).

title: Sprint Activity — redesigned activity history page
impact: The Work Item Activity page is renamed to Sprint Activity and now classifies each event by change type (Workflow/Scope/Creation/Metadata/Structure), collapses creation events into a single row, groups events by work item with collapse/expand support, and shows an Activity Summary block with six sprint-level metrics at the top of the page.

title: Sprint Delivery — epic table and feature modal improvements
impact: Sprint Delivery page now shows delivery signals clearly: products without activity are hidden; collapsed product sections show a compact summary (Delivered pts, Δ Effort pts, PBIs, Bugs); the epic table has new columns Delivered (pts) and Δ Effort (pts, absolute scope change) replacing Sprint Scored and Sprint Δ%; the feature drill-down modal also uses the same column structure; Sprint Δ% removed everywhere; feature progress bars are thinner and dimmer to reinforce the Epic → Feature → Activity hierarchy.

title: Portfolio Delivery — implemented aggregated delivery snapshot page
impact: Implemented the Portfolio Delivery page at /home/delivery/portfolio (Delivery workspace). The placeholder has been replaced with a fully functional page: sprint range selector (team + From/To sprint popovers with 5-sprint auto-default), portfolio summary metrics (Completed PBIs, Effort Delivered, Avg. Progress %, Bugs Created/Worked/Closed), product contribution horizontal bar chart (effort share per product), feature contribution horizontal bar chart (top 10 features by delivered effort), and bug distribution table (Created/Worked/Closed/Net per product). Works for single sprint or multi-sprint aggregated snapshot. No time-series charts — all visualisations show composition or distribution. Backend: new GET api/Metrics/portfolio-delivery endpoint, GetPortfolioDeliveryQuery, GetPortfolioDeliveryQueryHandler, PortfolioDeliveryDto/PortfolioDeliverySummaryDto/ProductDeliveryDto/FeatureDeliveryDto DTOs. IMetricsClient.GetPortfolioDeliveryAsync added to ApiClient.g.cs. DeliveryWorkspace 'Coming soon' chip removed. NAVIGATION_MAP.md and GEBRUIKERSHANDLEIDING.md updated.

title: Delivery Trends — new page for multi-sprint delivery analysis
impact: Added a dedicated Delivery Trends page at /home/trends/delivery under the Trends workspace. The page provides four sprint-timeline charts: PBI Throughput Trend (completed PBIs per sprint, primary full-width bar chart), Effort Throughput Trend (story points delivered per sprint), Progress Trend (completed effort as % of planned effort per sprint), and Bug Trend (bugs created vs closed per sprint). All charts follow the AnalyticalPageLayout / PrimaryVisualizationSection / SecondaryVisualizationGrid pattern with TrendSlopeBadge slope indicators. A collapsible drill-down table shows per-sprint detail. A signal card for Delivery Trends has been added to the Trends workspace. NAVIGATION_MAP.md and GEBRUIKERSHANDLEIDING.md updated to include the new page.

PrOverview.razor (/home/pull-requests) migrated from inline 2×2 CSS grid to AnalyticalPageLayout / PrimaryVisualizationSection / SecondaryVisualizationGrid. Median PR Size per Sprint is now the full-width primary visualization (340px chart); Median Time to First Review, Median Time to Merge, and P90 Time to Merge are arranged in a 3-column secondary grid (260px each). Page-local .pr-trend-grid <style> block removed. No logic, data, or density changes.

title: Sprint Trend and Pipeline Trend — migrated to Primary Visualization Pattern
impact: Sprint Trend (advanced/trend mode) and Pipeline Trend pages migrated from page-local inline CSS grids to the global AnalyticalPageLayout / PrimaryVisualizationSection / SecondaryVisualizationGrid components. Pipeline Trend: Reliability Trend is now the full-width primary visualization (340px chart); Time-to-Green, Tail Risk, and Flakiness are arranged in a 3-column secondary grid (260px each). Sprint Trend (trend mode): Progression per Sprint is the full-width primary visualization (340px); Completed PBIs Count, Completed PBI Effort, and Bug Activity are arranged in a 3-column secondary grid (260px each). Inline min-height pixel values and page-local grid CSS blocks removed. No logic, data, or density changes.

title: Primary Visualization Pattern — global analytical layout components and Portfolio Progress Trend refactor
impact: Introduced three reusable Blazor components (AnalyticalPageLayout, PrimaryVisualizationSection, SecondaryVisualizationGrid) implementing the new Primary Visualization Pattern for analytical pages. CSS tokens --analytical-primary-height (400px) and --analytical-secondary-height (320px) added to app.css. Portfolio Progress Trend page refactored: Combined Flow is now a full-width primary visualization (Row 1, min 400px, chart 340px tall) and Total Scope / Remaining Effort / Throughput are arranged in a 3-column secondary grid (Row 2, min 320px, chart 260px each). The old 2×2 symmetric grid is removed. No density rules altered; filter collapse behaviour preserved. UI_RULES.md §16 documents the pattern as binding for all future analytical pages.

title: Portfolio Progress Trend — graph semantics, neutral slope wording, and collapsible filters
impact: Combined Flow chart changed from Line to Bar (flow metrics are discrete per-sprint quantities). Total Scope chart changed from Bar to Line (stock metric showing accumulated state). Slope badges now use neutral system-dynamics terminology: Net Flow → Contracting/Expanding/Balanced; Scope & Remaining → Decreasing/Increasing/Stable; Throughput → Rising/Falling/Stable. Filter panel is now collapsed by default; context chips (Product, Team, Sprint range, Custom Scope badge) are always visible under the header showing the active scope without needing to open filters. TrendSlopeBadge component extended with optional PositiveLabel/NegativeLabel/NeutralLabel parameters for per-metric custom wording (fully backward-compatible).

title: Portfolio Progress Trend — historical per-sprint scope tracking via activity ledger
impact: TotalScopeEffort is now computed historically for each sprint in the selected range instead of using a single current-state snapshot. The handler replays Microsoft.VSTS.Scheduling.Effort change events from the ActivityEventLedger to reconstruct the total backlog scope as it was at each sprint's end date. Items created after a sprint's end are excluded; items currently in 'Removed' state that were removed after a sprint's end are included. TotalScopeChangePts in the summary now reflects actual scope growth or contraction across the selected range. Accuracy improves as more history accumulates in the ledger.

title: Portfolio Progress Trend — stock-and-flow model with Combined Flow graph
impact: The Portfolio Progress Trend page has been refactored from a % Done / scope snapshot view into a stock-and-flow product-dynamics model. Graph 1 is now a Combined Flow chart showing Completed (green), Added (orange, proxy from PlannedEffort), and Net Flow (blue, dominant) per sprint — Net > 0 means the backlog is shrinking. Graphs 2–4 retain Total Scope (Stock), Remaining Effort (Stock), and Throughput Trend. The summary block now shows Cumulative Net Flow, Total Scope Change, Remaining Effort Change, and classifies the range as Contracting / Stable / Expanding. The page auto-loads with All Products, the first available team, and the current sprint + past 4 sprints (5 total) as the default selection. The % Done graph has been removed. A documented limitation note explains that Added Effort uses PlannedEffort as an inflow proxy and cannot distinguish newly created items from re-estimations.

title: Pipeline Trend — Sprint-based PO view replaces Pipeline Insights
impact: The /home/pipelines page has been redesigned from an engineering telemetry table (single-sprint KPI tiles and stacked charts) into a sprint-bucketed PO trend page. It now shows 4 signals in a compact 2×2 grid: Reliability Trend (success rate %), Time-to-Green Trend (median build duration on main/master branch when data is available; otherwise all-branch fallback), Tail Risk Trend (P90 duration, gaps shown for fewer than 3 runs), and Flakiness/Instability Trend. Each chart includes a slope badge (Improving / Stable / Worsening). A sprint range selector (team, product, end sprint, count) replaces the single-sprint/multi-sprint toggle. A collapsed Advanced/Drill-down panel retains per-sprint detail including P75 and main-branch run counts.

title: Velocity Trend cleanup — signals embedded in Sprint Trend & Planning
impact: The standalone Velocity Trend tile has been removed from Trends. Velocity distribution (median, P25–P75 band) and predictability ratio are now embedded directly in Sprint Trend (Calibration panel, visible in advanced multi-sprint mode) and Planning (Capacity Confidence block). The Trends page is now more focused; calibration signals appear in their tactical and forecasting contexts.
